author: @Warriorking.1963
text:
I don't know what the law is regarding stills in England, but in Northern Ireland - where I am - if you get caught even in possession of one, and it's off to jail for YOU m'lad!

One local museum actually has a still on display, and even just for that, they had to get special licences and permission, as it could be used to make Poitin (Irish Moonshine).

So, while I really enjoyed your video as always, I think I won't be rushing out get a pressure cooker and some copper pipe.

author: @joetuktyyuktuk8635
text: My welding instructor way back when, used to work at the Hiram Walker Distillery. One day he decided to give a demo on the proper way to make a still, should any of us be inclined to do so, sometime in our lives. #1 he insisted that there be at least 37" from the surface of your boiling mash, to the start of your condensing coil, or a contaminated form of alcohol could result that causes blindness.

author: @tonyoliver4920
text:
In the uk it is completely legal to distill alcohol. 
As long as it’s not for human consumption. 
So you burned the alcohol - that’s biofuel and HMRC aren’t coming knocking at your door
Hand sanitizer - important in today’s health climate - would also be completely legal

If you actually want to drink the alcohol you distilled, you need a rectifiers license. But, you can only distill duty paid spirits. And I’ve heard you can no longer make home brew in the licensed premises. However, I haven’t seen any legislation that supports this.

author: @TileBitan
text: You are wrong about the alcohol & water. They form an azeotrope, which means that you can't boil the alcohol without dragging some water. The trick to purify is that the evaporated mixture is richer in alcohol than it was before, so if you repeat the destillation on the new mixture you can purify more and more until you reach the asyntotic limit (i dont remember the name), which is NOT 100% alcohol, but a bit less

author: @budove58
text: Just a small correction.  Distilling alcohol is not illegal in some states in the US.  For instance in Missouri you can legally distill the same amount of beverage that you can brew legally which is 200 gallons per year per adult in the household.  I know, that's a crazy amount of distilled alcohol but the point is making moonshine or other distilled spirits is perfectly legal in some states.

author: @WalterBurton
text:
I don't think it's illegal in America. Well, each state probably has its own laws. I'm a Virginian. I imagine we have laws that limit the quantity you can make or sell or something. We're pretty pragmatic that way, compared to a lot of other states. But I honestly don't know. Maybe I'll look it up? I'll come back and edit this comment if I do.

EDIT: Ah. Should've guessed. We charge you a license fee. USD$65 if you want a still for personal use.

author: @ile84

author: @michaelschauperl172
text:
I tried to bend a straight copper pipe filled with stuffed sand,  to a coil, for a rockeoven, cookingstove water heater., it didn't work for me. I compeedly took my motivaton. . . I wish I had known that there
 ones that are desingned to be bend.
Maybe I should give it another go.

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: I would. I find rubbing the surface of the tubing warms it up and it bends nicely.  Thumb between middle and index finger. Rub the inside of the coil with your thumb until it feels warm. Then continue to work the warmth down the tube as you gently bend the tube to take the shape of the form. Last time I bought copper tubing from the hardware store a 10 foot piece was $9.49 and the 20 foot piece was $9.99. If you are trying to bend rigid copper pipe they have a tool for bending those pipes. Won't get tight radious circles, but it does bend with a long lever tool.

author: @petergambier
text:
Thanks Robert, it's always very satisfactory when we make things and making fuel or booze would be brilliant. 

I also have a 2001 Citroen Berlingo, are there many adjustments needed for the engine and what would be optimum to condense down do you reckon?

It was also funny seeing your assistant trying to look like he was meant to be there and, guessing he is the heir to the family overdraft, does he do sciency things like dad or has he gone down a different path?

I enjoy reading through the comments, they can lead down a right old rabbit hole of wonder, first I had to look up what azetrope (azeotrope?) then Raoult's law and mole fractions , non of which I'd ever heard, the only science I do is to slakes my own putty from quick lime for small jobs and never concentrated much during science lessons at school, most of my science teachers were pants at teaching so how can you expect to learn?

Always enjoyed watching your films because they are very educational thanks.

By the by, there's a missing Q at 0:15.

Live long and prosper.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Sweet... but I think you'll get better separation of water from the "bio fuel". Something close to azetropic  if you have the vapors rise through a condensing coil that encourages water to condense out and fall back in.  Something like reflux distillation. 

 Then a run to a colder condensing coil that allows the remaining condensed fluids to drip out and be collected. 😁

To be effective this requires  observing the temperature at the top of the distillation setup and adjusting the heat slowly up so that you get just the temp at the top to minimize water  coming over.

author: @gordonlawrence1448
text: Vacuum distillation is fascinating. You can make a very basic and simple vacuum pump by reversing the seals in an old style bicycle pump and reversing a presta valve. At room temperature ethanol boils at somewhere between 0,1 and 0.05 bar. Get the condenser and receiver down to 10C and you would be laughing.
